summary: Gruber dissects reports that Google was caught off guard by Apple's decision to replace Google Maps with its own mapping service in iOS 6, a year before their contract expired. He lays out Apple's three options — stick with inferior maps for another year, renegotiate with Google on unfavorable terms, or build their own — and argues it was obvious Apple would choose independence. He finds Google's claimed surprise implausible given that the entire tech industry had anticipated this move for years. The piece suggests Google is strategically playing the victim to shape the narrative around Apple Maps' rocky launch.
key_points: Apple ended its Google Maps agreement a year early, choosing to ship its own mapping service despite known data quality gaps
Apple's alternatives to building its own maps — staying a year behind competitors or granting Google more branding and user data access — were both unacceptable
Google's claim of being caught off guard is implausible given the entire tech industry expected Apple to build its own maps for years
The reporting suggests Google is strategically leaking a 'surprised' narrative to shape public perception during Apple Maps' troubled launch
Google's stated demands for renewal — more branding, Latitude integration — would have given Apple's archrival more control and access to user data on Apple's platform
money_quotes: We're supposed to believe that Google was surprised that Apple left the deal a year early? That I don't buy. It implies that Google's executives are either stupid, lying, or blindingly arrogant.

So Apple's options were: Continue for one more year with iOS Maps as it was — no turn-by-turn directions, no vector-based map tiles — and thus go for another year at significant feature disadvantage to all other major phones on the market.
More Google branding, less user privacy.

Cut the cord with Google now and go ahead with Apple's own mapping services — so that they can add turn-by-turn directions, greatly improve the aesthetics of the map graphics, use vector map tiles, and, most importantly, take control of their own destiny.
Everyone else in the tech world expected Apple to release its own mapping service for three years, but Google, the one company that stood to lose the most, did not? OK, sure.
If you believe that, I have a melted bridge to sell you.
Google is so out-of-touch that it truly did not see how unappealing options #1 and especially #2 would be to Apple.
key_insight: Google's claimed surprise at Apple building its own maps is either strategic narrative management or a damning admission that Google couldn't see how unacceptable its own renewal terms were to Apple.
